title: Fail to build Dockerfile: unpacking of archive failed on file /etc/hosts: cpio: rename
body:
<!--
If you are reporting a new issue, make sure that we do not have any duplicates
already open. You can ensure this by searching the issue list for this
repository. If there is a duplicate, please close your issue and add a comment
to the existing issue instead.

If you suspect your issue is a bug, please edit your issue description to
include the BUG REPORT INFORMATION shown below. If you fail to provide this
information within 7 days, we cannot debug your issue and will close it. We
will, however, reopen it if you later provide the information.

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST
-->

**Description**

I am trying to build a Dockerfile. It builds ok with Docker but fails with buildah.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. Dockerfile: http://pastebin.test.redhat.com/652536
2. `podman build -t install-test .`


**Describe the results you received:**
Error logs: http://pastebin.test.redhat.com/652525

**Describe the results you expected:**
Expected it to build (works with Docker).


**Output of `rpm -q buildah` or `apt list buildah`:**

```
buildah-1.3-1.git4888163.fc28.x86_64
```

**Output of `buildah version`:**

```
Version:         1.3
Go Version:      go1.10.3
Image Spec:      1.0.0
Runtime Spec:    1.0.0
CNI Spec:        0.3.1
libcni Version:  v0.6.0
Git Commit:      4888163
Built:           Sun Aug  5 07:31:55 2018
OS/Arch:         linux/amd64
```


**Output of `cat /etc/*release`:**

```
Fedora release 28 (Twenty Eight)
NAME=Fedora
VERSION="28 (Workstation Edition)"
ID=fedora
VERSION_ID=28
PLATFORM_ID="platform:f28"
PRETTY_NAME="Fedora 28 (Workstation Edition)"
ANSI_COLOR="0;34"
CPE_NAME="cpe:/o:fedoraproject:fedora:28"
HOME_URL="https://fedoraproject.org/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Communicating_and_getting_help"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugzilla.redhat.com/"
REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT="Fedora"
REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT_VERSION=28
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Fedora"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION=28
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Legal:PrivacyPolicy"
VARIANT="Workstation Edition"
VARIANT_ID=workstation
Fedora release 28 (Twenty Eight)
Fedora release 28 (Twenty Eight)
```

**Output of `uname -a`:**

```
Linux localhost.localdomain 4.18.5-200.fc28.x86_64 #1 SMP Tue Sep 4 15:56:14 UTC 2018 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

**Output of `cat /etc/containers/storage.conf`:**

```
# This file is is the configuration file for all tools
# that use the containers/storage library.
# See man 5 containers-storage.conf for more information
# The "container storage" table contains all of the server options.
[storage]

# Default Storage Driver
driver = "overlay"

# Temporary storage location
runroot = "/var/run/containers/storage"

# Primary Read/Write location of container storage
graphroot = "/var/lib/containers/storage"

[storage.options]
# Storage options to be passed to underlying storage drivers

# AdditionalImageStores is used to pass paths to additional Read/Only image stores
# Must be comma separated list.
additionalimagestores = [
]

# Size is used to set a maximum size of the container image.  Only supported by
# certain container storage drivers.
size = ""

# Path to an helper program to use for mounting the file system instead of mounting it
# directly.
#mount_program = "/usr/bin/fuse-overlayfs"

# OverrideKernelCheck tells the driver to ignore kernel checks based on kernel version
override_kernel_check = "true"

# mountopt specifies comma separated list of extra mount options
mountopt = "nodev"

# Remap-UIDs/GIDs is the mapping from UIDs/GIDs as they should appear inside of
# a container, to UIDs/GIDs as they should appear outside of the container, and
# the length of the range of UIDs/GIDs.  Additional mapped sets can be listed
# and will be heeded by libraries, but there are limits to the number of
# mappings which the kernel will allow when you later attempt to run a
# container.
#
# remap-uids = 0:1668442479:65536
# remap-gids = 0:1668442479:65536

# Remap-User/Group is a name which can be used to look up one or more UID/GID
# ranges in the /etc/subuid or /etc/subgid file.  Mappings are set up starting
# with an in-container ID of 0 and the a host-level ID taken from the lowest
# range that matches the specified name, and using the length of that range.
# Additional ranges are then assigned, using the ranges which specify the
# lowest host-level IDs first, to the lowest not-yet-mapped container-level ID,
# until all of the entries have been used for maps.
#
# remap-user = "storage"
# remap-group = "storage"

[storage.options.thinpool]
# Storage Options for thinpool

# autoextend_percent determines the amount by which pool needs to be
# grown. This is specified in terms of % of pool size. So a value of 20 means
# that when threshold is hit, pool will be grown by 20% of existing
# pool size.
# autoextend_percent = "20"

# autoextend_threshold determines the pool extension threshold in terms
# of percentage of pool size. For example, if threshold is 60, that means when
# pool is 60% full, threshold has been hit.
# autoextend_threshold = "80"

# basesize specifies the size to use when creating the base device, which
# limits the size of images and containers.
# basesize = "10G"

# blocksize specifies a custom blocksize to use for the thin pool.
# blocksize="64k"

# directlvm_device specifies a custom block storage device to use for the
# thin pool. Required if you setup devicemapper.
# directlvm_device = ""

# directlvm_device_force wipes device even if device already has a filesystem.
# directlvm_device_force = "True"

# fs specifies the filesystem type to use for the base device.
# fs="xfs"

# log_level sets the log level of devicemapper.
# 0: LogLevelSuppress 0 (Default)
# 2: LogLevelFatal
# 3: LogLevelErr
# 4: LogLevelWarn
# 5: LogLevelNotice
# 6: LogLevelInfo
# 7: LogLevelDebug
# log_level = "7"

# min_free_space specifies the min free space percent in a thin pool require for
# new device creation to succeed. Valid values are from 0% - 99%.
# Value 0% disables
# min_free_space = "10%"

# mkfsarg specifies extra mkfs arguments to be used when creating the base.
# device.
# mkfsarg = ""

# use_deferred_removal marks devicemapper block device for deferred removal.
# If the thinpool is in use when the driver attempts to remove it, the driver 
# tells the kernel to remove it as soon as possible. Note this does not free
# up the disk space, use deferred deletion to fully remove the thinpool.
# use_deferred_removal = "True"

# use_deferred_deletion marks thinpool device for deferred deletion.
# If the device is busy when the driver attempts to delete it, the driver
# will attempt to delete device every 30 seconds until successful.
# If the program using the driver exits, the driver will continue attempting
# to cleanup the next time the driver is used. Deferred deletion permanently
# deletes the device and all data stored in device will be lost.
# use_deferred_deletion = "True"

# xfs_nospace_max_retries specifies the maximum number of retries XFS should
# attempt to complete IO when ENOSPC (no space) error is returned by
# underlying storage device.
# xfs_nospace_max_retries = "0"

# If specified, use OSTree to deduplicate files with the overlay backend
ostree_repo = ""

# Set to skip a PRIVATE bind mount on the storage home directory.  Only supported by
# certain container storage drivers
skip_mount_home = "false"
```

comments.body: @patrickdillon Thanks for the problem report.  This looks to be  a problem with yum not being able to find a package from the repository.  When you ran this in Docker, did you run it on the same machine that you ran the buildah bud command from?
Yes. I ran both commands in the same shell session.
@TomSweeneyRedHat Could you see if this works for you?
@rhatdan ack, will give it a go Monday if not before.
@rhatdan FYI, I'm able to reproduce this from master, looking.

/etc/hosts is a volume mount into the container.  It is probably conflicting on that.

One big difference between the two is Docker puts /etc/hosts on a file system:
/dev/mapper/fedora-var on /etc/hosts type xfs (rw,relatime,seclabel,attr2,inode64,prjquota)

While Buildah has it on a tmpfs?
tmpfs on /etc/hosts type tmpfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,seclabel)

I tried running the RPM command within the container and I think you've hit the why @rhatdan I'm seeing an error on /etc/hosts there:

```
Package
       Arch   Version       Repository                                                                           Size
======================================================================================================================
Updating:
 setup noarch 2.8.71-10.el7 download-node-02.eng.bos.redhat.com_rel-eng_latest-RHEL-7_compose_Server_x86_64_os_ 166 k

Transaction Summary
======================================================================================================================
Upgrade  1 Package

Total download size: 166 k
Downloading packages:
Delta RPMs disabled because /usr/bin/applydeltarpm not installed.
setup-2.8.71-10.el7.noarch.rpm                                                                 | 166 kB  00:00:02     
Running transaction check
Running transaction test
Transaction test succeeded
Running transaction
  Updating   : setup-2.8.71-10.el7.noarch                                                                         1/2 
Error unpacking rpm package setup-2.8.71-10.el7.noarch
error: unpacking of archive failed on file /etc/hosts: cpio: rename
  Verifying  : setup-2.8.71-10.el7.noarch                                                                         1/2 
setup-2.8.71-9.el7.noarch was supposed to be removed but is not!
  Verifying  : setup-2.8.71-9.el7.noarch                                                                          2/2 

Failed:
  setup.noarch 0:2.8.71-9.el7                               setup.noarch 0:2.8.71-10.el7                              

Complete!
```
Why this is different from Docker I don't know.
@nalind WDYT?
@TomSweeneyRedHat Do we still have this issue?
IDK, I'll retest.  I know we've made changes to the way we handle /etc/host in v1.5, hopefully this got cleansed with those changes.

This is actually looking much better.  It got past Step 3 where it failed before and failed in Step 6 try to copy ssh config around.  @patrickdillon Could you by chance try the test again with the latest Buildah (v1.5).

```
{Removed a ton of happy output}
STEP 4: ENV ANSIBLE_STDOUT_CALLBACK yaml
STEP 5: ENV ANSIBLE_STRATEGY debug
STEP 6: ADD configs/ssh-config /root/.ssh/config
error building at step {Env:[ANSIBLE_STRATEGY=debug ANSIBLE_STDOUT_CALLBACK=yaml PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin container=oci   ANSIBLE_STDOUT_CALLBACK=yaml ANSIBLE_STRATEGY=debug] Command:add Args:[configs/ssh-config /root/.ssh/config] Flags:[] Attrs:map[] Message:ADD configs/ssh-config /root/.ssh/config Original:ADD configs/ssh-config /root/.ssh/config}: no files found matching "/tmp/configs/ssh-config": no such file or directory
# 
```
That seems unrelated to the original issue?

@rhatdan yep, the error that I ran into was completely unrelated to the original issue.  I'm 99.999999% sure it's due to my test system not being set up in the same way as @patrickdillon 's as I didn't have a configs/ssh-config in the environment to ADD.
Ok I will claim this is fixed in buildah 1.5/

@TomSweeneyRedHat  I am hitting an issue in step 2.
I installed Buildah v1.5 from the repo:
```
$ buildah version
Version:         1.5
Go Version:      go1.11
Image Spec:      1.0.0
Runtime Spec:    1.0.0
CNI Spec:        0.4.0
libcni Version:  v0.7.0-alpha1
Git Commit:      e94b4f98
Built:           Wed Nov 28 16:08:54 2018
OS/Arch:         linux/amd64```
```

I reduced the Dockerfile: 
```
FROM registry.access.redhat.com/rhel7:latest

RUN yum-config-manager \
      --add-repo http://download-node-02.eng.bos.redhat.com/rel-eng/latest-RHEL-7/compose/Server/x86_64/os/ \
      --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ansible/2.6/os/ \
      --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ose/3.10/os/ \
      &>/dev/null && \
      yum-config-manager --save \
        --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" \
        --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0 \
        --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null
```
Docker builds:
```
$ docker build -t install-test .

.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ose/3.10/os/       &>/dev/null &&       yum-config-manager --save         --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///et
c/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release"         --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0         --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null                            
 ---> Using cache
 ---> 3b1021bb448f
Successfully built 3b1021bb448f
```
Buildah gives an error:
```
$ buildah bud -t install-test .
STEP 1: FROM registry.access.redhat.com/rhel7:latest

tp://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ansible/2.6/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.re
dhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ose/3.10/os/       &>/dev/null &&       yum-config-manager --save         --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/p
ki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release"         --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0         --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null                               
container exited on segmentation fault
error building at step {Env:[PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin container=oci] Command:run Args:[yum-config-manager       --add
-repo http://download-node-02.eng.bos.redhat.com/rel-eng/latest-RHEL-7/compose/Server/x86_64/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.c
om/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ansible/2.6/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Serve
r/x86_64/ose/3.10/os/       &>/dev/null &&       yum-config-manager --save         --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release"    
     --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0         --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null] Flags:[] Attrs:map[] Message:RUN yum-config-manager       --add-repo htt
p://download-node-02.eng.bos.redhat.com/rel-eng/latest-RHEL-7/compose/Server/x86_64/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/conten
t/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ansible/2.6/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/
ose/3.10/os/       &>/dev/null &&       yum-config-manager --save         --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release"         --se
topt=\*.gpgcheck=0         --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null Original:RUN yum-config-manager       --add-repo http://download-node-02.eng.bos.r
edhat.com/rel-eng/latest-RHEL-7/compose/Server/x86_64/os/       --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x

 &&       yum-config-manager --save         --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release"         --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0         --s
etopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null}: error while running runtime: exit status 1                                                                   
ERRO[0000] exit status 1
```
Thanks for the tests @patrickdillon and drats, I'd hoped it been fixed.  I've reopened the issue.
Oh, and thanks for the shorter reproducer!
So this is still the /etc/hosts issue?

@rhatdan I don't think it's /etc/hosts, but I'm not sure what's going on.  I'm not able to reproduce it on Fedora 29 with the latest Buildah from upstream.  I did note that when I ran build complained about HOSTNAME being supported only in Docker format, not sure where that picked up from.  

Could there be a difference in F28 and F29 that's causing this for @patrickdillon and not me?

Here's my tests runs both that worked although the first squawked about the HOSTNAME.

```
# cat ~/Dockerfile
FROM registry.access.redhat.com/rhel7:latest

RUN yum-config-manager \
      --add-repo http://download-node-02.eng.bos.redhat.com/rel-eng/latest-RHEL-7/compose/Server/x86_64/os/ \
      --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ansible/2.6/os/ \
      --add-repo http://pulp-read.dist.prod.ext.phx2.redhat.com/content/dist/rhel/server/7/7Server/x86_64/ose/3.10/os/ \
      &>/dev/null && \

        --setopt=\*.gpgkey="file:///etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" \
        --setopt=\*.gpgcheck=0 \
        --setopt=\*.skip_if_unavailable=true &>/dev/null

Oh just noted, looks like you're running as non-root, is that correct @patrickdillon ?  I ran as root and it worked fine for me.

@TomSweeneyRedHat Apologies for the confusion: yes, sudo, progressed the build. 
My build actually still fails on step 3, but docker --no-cache fails identically, so I think there is a problem with the dockerfile or package. I'm closing this, unless you think I can provide any more useful information.

I can verify this on RHEL8 HTB Snapshot 2, with podman-1.0.0-1.git82e8011.module+el8+2696+e59f0461.x86_64 and this Dockerfile:

```
FROM registry.centos.org/che-stacks/centos-stack-base

RUN sudo yum -y update setup

CMD tail -f /dev/null 
```
Which fails with:
```
Running transaction
  Updating   : setup-2.8.71-10.el7.noarch                                   1/2

Perhaps a wild goose chase, but difference from an initial failed run on a newly installed server, to the first successful run, is:
```
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/backingFsBlockDev
```
And
```
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/l/FJFIQ2BZGRFR7GJAMCF3763
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/720ac4c54619badde0815b53b
...
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay/c5219d6d53f9b7f0ad9a79296
...
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-layers/720ac4c54619badde0
...
/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-images/aaba8f7a6ec631f281
```

Creation of backingFsBlockDev seems to live in: vendor/github.com/containers/storage/drivers/quota/projectquota.go
```
// projectquota.go - implements XFS project quota controls
// for setting quota limits on a newly created directory.
// It currently supports the legacy XFS specific ioctls.
```

I did a replicator where I install two new RHEL 8 HTB snap4 instances, and only once run:
podman build --log-level debug -t podman:test .
On one machine, I implemented the work around, on one not, before running the commands, so the output should be very similar and point at what is different. 

Here's the debug output, attached. Perhaps it contains something to move this forward.
podman-build-ok.txt and podman-build-fail.txt containers the result of the command, in full. In the *-debug-msg.txt files I filtered out only debug messages.

[podman-build-fail-debug-msg.txt](https://github.com/containers/buildah/files/2793711/podman-build-fail-debug-msg.txt)
[podman-build-ok-debug-msg.txt](https://github.com/containers/buildah/files/2793712/podman-build-ok-debug-msg.txt)
[podman-build-ok.txt](https://github.com/containers/buildah/files/2793713/podman-build-ok.txt)
[podman-build-fail.txt](https://github.com/containers/buildah/files/2793714/podman-build-fail.txt)

Looking at the differences between ok build and failed build, and specifically on DEBU[n] messages, the failed build displays messages about "DEBU[0002] Detected compression format gzip" which the ok build does not. Perhaps that is clue that the problem is triggered by a race condition. I'll be online tomorrow if you need more info from my test beds.

If it works on docker, but not on podman, and as I could test - the problem only appears if hostname does not have an entry in /etc/hosts on the machine you build on - then perhaps the issue is related to https://github.com/containers/image/issues/72. Just a thought.

Worth to note is that there are a lot of systems without a hostname entry in /etc/hosts, so this seems like something a lot of people will hit. At least on RHEL 8, where I can replicate this at will on a default install (RHEL does not by default create a hostname entry in /etc/hosts, probably also the case for most Linux OSs).

After reading all of these remarks, is the solution to the problem populating the hostname within the /etc/hosts that gets mounted into the container?

> After reading all of these remarks, is the solution to the problem populating the hostname within the /etc/hosts that gets mounted into the container?

Not sure how it works, but, putting the hostname into /etc/hosts of the OS instance running the container solves the problem.

The problem, I think is that the /etc/hosts file is a mount point, and the setup install is trying to rename it.
```
# buildah run alpine-working-container sh
/ # mv /etc/hosts /etc/hosts1
mv: can't rename '/etc/hosts': Resource busy
```

Same thing happens with Docker and Podman.
error: unpacking of archive failed on file /etc/hosts: cpio: rename
@rhatdan I agree with the /etc/host being a mount point and setup trying to rename it as the probable issue.  Not sure how to get around that though.
@rhvgoyal Any ideas?

@rhatdan @TomasTomecek, can it perhaps be like this?

Known: The setup RPM contains /etc/hosts. 

Guessworks: AFAIKS, the RPM includes no scripts which does things with /etc/hosts. 

Known: RPM should not replace any existing files, by default at least. 

Question: So, why does setup then touch /etc/hosts?

Guessworks: I suppose that is because it does not see /etc/hosts, and tries to put the file in place - which then does not work.

But, when I add the FQDN to /etc/hosts, on the server which builds the container, setup inside of the container perhaps sees /etc/hosts, and then it does not try to replace or add the file - therefor there are no problems?

Hypothetic question: Why does the setup RPM only see /etc/hosts when I've added the FQDN to /etc/hosts on the server which builds the container?

[root@rhel8htb ~]# cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1   localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1         localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
Is this after you edit it?

@rhatdan no, that's how it looks before I edit it.

It looks like this after I've edited it:
[root@rhel8htb ~]# cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1 localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1 localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
123.456.678.9 rhel8htb rhel8htb.localdomain

@rhatdan strangely enough, I now found this:

If I build the container with below:
```
FROM registry.centos.org/che-stacks/centos-stack-base
RUN if [ -f /etc/hosts]; then echo no; else yum -y update setup; fi
CMD tail -f /dev/null
```
Then the RPM does not get installed and a container get's built without issues. So, if the problem is that setup cannot see /etc/hosts, it has some other view than when that evaluation is run. If I then re-build the container with:
```
FROM registry.centos.org/che-stacks/centos-stack-base
RUN yum -y update setup
CMD tail -f /dev/null
```
There are no problems and setup get's updated, even without doing anything to /etc/hosts on my build host.

Please note that once I've gotten past this issue, I cannot replicate the problem anymore.

Looking at setup.conf, it lists /etc/hosts as
%config(noreplace) /etc/host.conf

Which means it checks to see if the config has been modified from the default, and if so it does not replace it.  If the /etc/host.conf has not been changed then it will replace it.  

This is why when you modify the /etc/hosts from the system setup.conf does not change it.

What we could do is modify the hosts file that we are mounting into the container that the hosts came from inside of a container, and then setup.conf will no longer attempt to replace it.
This should be fixed now.
